short_explanation: The broadest everyday group: plant animal or fungus.
more_info: Kingdom sits just below domain in modern taxonomy. Life is split into groups like Animalia (animals) Plantae (plants) and Fungi (fungi). Near Nature's first classifier step predicts kingdom (plus not-organism) to route a photo into the plant or animal pipeline. A single photo is always one kingdom.
example: American Robin → Animalia; White Oak → Plantae
near_nature_usage: First routing step after scene rejection; kingdom model outputs plantae animalia fungi uncertain or not_organism

short_explanation: Major body-plan division within a kingdom.
more_info: Phylum groups organisms that share a very deep ancestral blueprint. In animals examples include Chordata (backboned animals) Arthropoda (insects and spiders) and Mollusca (snails and clams). In plants the phylum level is often less visible to users because flowering plants are grouped under vascular-plant lineages.
example: Bald Eagle → Chordata; Monarch Butterfly → Arthropoda
near_nature_usage: Stored in catalog rows for provenance and filtering; not a primary user-facing label in v5 specialists

short_explanation: Large group united by fundamental structure and ancestry.
more_info: Class is more specific than phylum. Animal classes include Aves (birds) Mammalia (mammals) Insecta (insects) and Reptilia (reptiles). Plant classes include groups like Magnoliopsida (flowering dicots). Class helps Near Nature assign observations to specialist pipelines such as birds or lepidoptera.
example: Painted Bunting → Aves; Gopher Tortoise → Reptilia
near_nature_usage: Drives specialist_group routing (e.g. Aves → birds Lepidoptera order → lepidoptera)

short_explanation: Related families bundled into a larger natural cluster.
more_info: Order groups families that share a more recent common ancestor. Examples include Passeriformes (perching birds) Lepidoptera (butterflies and moths) and Fagales (oaks beeches and relatives). Orders are useful for science and data cleanup but Near Nature usually does not show order as the final identification label.
near_nature_usage: Used in ingest routing and label normalization; rarely shown as the final app label

short_explanation: Close relatives that share a common genus-level ancestor.
more_info: Family names end in -idae for animals (Ardeidae herons) or -aceae for plants (Fagaceae oaks and beeches). Near Nature trains most specialist models at family level because families are stable recognizable in photos and have enough training images. The app may show family as the scientific label when genus-level certainty is not available.
example: Great Blue Heron → Ardeidae; Southern Live Oak → Fagaceae
near_nature_usage: Primary production class for birds herps lepidoptera insects arachnids wildflowers ferns and many trees in v5

short_explanation: The first name in a scientific name; a group of closely related species.
more_info: Genus is always capitalized and shared by species that are more alike than any species outside the genus. The full species name combines genus plus species epithet (Homo sapiens Quercus virginiana). Near Nature uses genus as a seed target for data collection and as the display label when species-level IDs are not trained.
near_nature_usage: Seed lists in target_classes; gap-fill queries (GBIF); shown when species-level model is unavailable

short_explanation: The most specific common rank: one kind of naturally interbreeding organism.
more_info: Species is written as genus plus epithet (Canis familiaris Pinus palustris). It is the gold standard for identification but needs many labeled photos per species. Near Nature uses species labels for domestic mammals (e.g. Bos taurus) and for iNat-grade fine IDs when data volume supports it.
example: Dog → Canis familiaris; Longleaf Pine → Pinus palustris
near_nature_usage: Production label for domestic common_mammals (is_wild=0); target for future fine-grained models where data allows
